title: 怪钟疑案
summary:
Hercule Poirot discusses his methodology for solving the seemingly fantastical murder case with Colin Lamb. Poirot explains that the case's absurd and dramatic elements—resembling detective novels by authors like Agatha Christie or Dixon Carr—serve as distractions. He compares the situation to a woman hiding her aging face behind heavy makeup, jewelry, and furs; similarly, the strange designs of the crime distract from its essence.

Poirot asserts that to solve the murder, one must identify both the motive and the victim. He decides to treat the victim, Mr. Corry, as an ordinary, wealthy, and respected middle-aged man to narrow the search. He notes that while various people could be suspects—including Mrs. Hemm, the Brandt couple, the MacNodon couple, Miss Watrous, Miss Pemmerton, and Sheila Webb—the investigation must focus on why an ordinary man would need to die.

Poirot highlights a crucial observation made by the neighbor, Mrs. Hemm, who remarked that Mr. Corry "came here to die." This realization, combined with the fact that the victim had no identification, no wallet, and torn clothing labels, suggests a deliberate attempt to obscure his identity. Poirot notes that the identity of a man named "Curry" via an insurance agent's card may be a temporary measure. He further observes that the victim's lack of known associates and the absence of dental records suggest he may have been a foreign traveler whose disappearance went unnoticed.
